original_text: The Federal Reserve raises interest rates by 2% to combat inflation.
identified_property: effectiveness at combating inflation with minimal economic disruption

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: The Federal Reserve raises interest rates by 2% to combat inflation, but the abrupt hike triggers a severe recession and widespread unemployment while failing to meaningfully reduce inflation.

variations.label: low
variations.text: The Federal Reserve raises interest rates by 2% to combat inflation, but the poorly calibrated hike significantly slows economic growth and increases unemployment while only modestly curbing price pressures.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: The Federal Reserve raises interest rates by 2% to combat inflation, gradually reducing price pressures but causing a noticeable economic slowdown and measurable rise in unemployment.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: The Federal Reserve raises interest rates by 2% to combat inflation.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: The Federal Reserve raises interest rates by 2% to combat inflation, effectively curbing price increases while causing only a mild and short-lived economic slowdown.

variations.label: high
variations.text: The Federal Reserve raises interest rates by 2% to combat inflation, sharply reducing inflation toward its target while producing only minor, temporary effects on employment and economic growth.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: The Federal Reserve raises interest rates by 2% to combat inflation, swiftly restoring inflation to its target level while sustaining robust economic growth and stable employment throughout the adjustment.

quality_checks.reasoning: All tiers describe the same fundamental action (a 2% rate hike by the Fed). The property varied is the effectiveness at reducing inflation paired with the degree of economic disruption caused. Tier 1 features maximal disruption with minimal inflation reduction; each subsequent tier improves along both dimensions until Tier 7, which achieves full inflation control with essentially no economic cost.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because it represents the ideal monetary policy outcome: inflation is swiftly brought to target while the economy continues to grow and employment remains stable, achieving maximum effectiveness with minimal disruption.
